coredata.dc:title: Post-oxidation treated graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of copper ions
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yan
Liu, Xinqian
Li, Qiyao
Ge, Juan
Liu, Hui
Li, Sen
Wang, Lifeng
Wang, Jun
Ma, Ning
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been an ideal probe for copper ions detection due to its excellent optical properties, low toxicity and biocompatibility. However, most of GQDs prepared by present methods have low oxidation degree, which could hinder the complexation between copper ions and oxygen functional groups on the surface of GQDs. Therefore, we proposed a universal, mild and in situ post-treated approach for modifying GQDs obtained by any methods, which will increase the oxidation degree of GQDs without any other affect and its influence on the responsivity of copper ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Post-oxidation treatment
Fluorescent sensor
Copper ions detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21674011 , 21374009 , 51202011 ), Beijing Municipal Natural Science Foundation ( 2132049 ), Beijing Organization Department Outstanding Talented Person Project ( 2013D009006000001 ) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( FRF-TP-15-005A3 ). Yan Li is thankful for the financial support from the program of the China Scholarship Council (CSC).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The PL emissions of GQDs (a) and the post-treated GQDs with 5μL (b), 10μL (c), 20μL (d), 30μL (e), and 40μL (f) K2S2O8, respectively, under 365nm UV irradiation.
TEM images of the pristine GQDs (a) and T-GQDs (b). The histograms of size distributions for both samples are shown in the inserts of (a) and (b), respectively.
The XPS survey spectra (a) and the FT-IR spectra (b) as well as the Raman spectra (c) of the pristine GQDs and T-GQDs.
The UV–visible absorption spectra (a) and the PL spectra of the pristine GQDs (b) and T-GQDs (c), respectively.
The deconvoluted PL spectrum of GQD (a–d) and T-GQD (e–h) as the excitation wavelength increased from 260nm to 380nm, respectively.

(a) PL spectra of T-GQDs in the presence of Cu2+ (from up to down, the concentration of Cu2+ is 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50, 70, 90, 110, 130μM, respectively). (b) Fluorescence intensity response of T-GQDs to the concentration Cu2+ (inset: fluorescence intensity of T-GQDs versus the Cu2+ concentrations from 0 to 20μM).
The Stem-Volmer plots of the fluorescence quenching of T-GQDs in aqueous solution by Cu2+.

The various fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) of the T-GQDs solution in the absence and presence of various individual metal ions. F0 and Fare the fluorescence intensity at 340nm in the absence and presence of ions, respectively.

head.ce:title: Post-oxidation treated graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of copper ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Material Science and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Engineering University, Harbin 150001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Material Science and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been an ideal probe for copper ions detection due to its excellent optical properties, low toxicity and biocompatibility. However, most of GQDs prepared by present methods have low oxidation degree, which could hinder the complexation between copper ions and oxygen functional groups on the surface of GQDs. Therefore, we proposed a universal, mild and in situ post-treated approach for modifying GQDs obtained by any methods, which will increase the oxidation degree of GQDs without any other affect and its influence on the responsivity of copper ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, fluorescent chemosensors have served as useful tools for metal ions detection owing to their intrinsic sensitivity, selectivity and capacity for rapid, real-time monitoring [1–3]. In the detection of various metal ions, copper ions (Cu2+) detection has attracted wide attention since copper is a kind of necessary trace element for the organism and its concentration directly affects people health [4]. For that, The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has set the limit of copper in drinking water and the average concentration of blood copper in the normal group to be ∼20μM and 15.7–23.6μM, respectively [5,6]. Nowadays, numerous methods for the detection of Cu2+ have been proposed, including fluorescent probe sensors based on organic dyes/organic complex [7–10], carbon dots [11], CdSe-ZnS SQD [12], etc., ion mass spectrometry such as atomic absorption spectrometry [13], inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy [14], and electrochemical methods [15]. However, most of these methods need high-cost equipment, complex and time-consuming procedures, and even using toxic reagents. Therefore, it is quite necessary to develop a simple, low-cost and promising method which can rapidly track the level of Cu2+ in biological and environmental samples.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a new fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterial, have emerged and ignited tremendous research interest. Due to the pronounced quantum confinement and edge effects, GQDs assume numerous novel chemical/physical properties [16,17]. Besides, GQDs also show low cytotoxicity, excellent solubility, chemical inertia, stable photoluminescence, better surface grafting, thus making them promising in optoelectronic devices, sensors, bioimaging, etc. [16–21]. Of date, there are some studies that have reported about the application of GQDs in detecting Cu2+, among of which, the detection mechanism in Lin’s report is that the carboxyl groups on the surface of Eu-modified GQDs complex with Cu2+, and thus the energy transfer between them lead to the quenched fluorescence of GQDs, determining the concentration of Cu2+ according the reduced level of fluorescence intensity [22]. Based on such mechanism, it seems that the more carboxyl groups exist on the surface of GQDs, the more active sites for the combination of Cu2+ and GQDs can be produced. Thus, if there is one method which can modify more carboxyl groups on GQDs’ surface, the efficiency of Cu2+ detection can be improved significantly.

High purity graphite rods were purchased from Beijing auspicious prosperity industry and trade co., Ltd. All solvents and reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd and used without further purification.

GQDs were prepared by electrochemical cyclic voltammetry technology using a CHI660D electrochemical workstation, as described in our previous reports [24]. Graphite rod, platinum electrode, and Ag/AgCl were used as the working, counter, and reference electrodes, respectively. 0.1M phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solution with a pH value of 7.0 was used as the electrolyte. The applied potential window was set to −5.0 to 5.0V, the scan rate was 0.5V/s, and continuous conducted 129600cycles. After filtering by a 220nm filter membrane and dialyzing with a cellulose ester membrane bag (retained molecular weight: 3500Da) for 6days, GQDs aqueous solutions were obtained, which were used for further oxidation treatments.

An aqueous solution of GQDs (20mL) was poured into a clean beaker, and then 20μL (0.1M) of K2S2O8 solution was dropped into the solution by a pipette. A small amount of K2S2O8 (ca. 0.1vol% of total volume) ensured that the concentration of GQDs maintained the same (0.05mg/ml) during the post-oxidation process. After stirring for 1min, the mixture solution was exposed to UV irradiation with a wavelength of 365nm, which was provided by an 18W UV lamp. The distance between the mixture solution and UV light was 0.5cm, and the exposure time was 72h. The treated GQDs were named as T-GQDs. Once T-GQDs were obtained, a dialysis process was carried out immediately to remove excess K2S2O8. The dialysis process lasted for 4days.

Stock standard solution 0.1M Cu2+ was prepared by dissolving an appropriate amount of Cu(NO3)2 in deionized water. Then a fixed concentration of T-GQDs was transferred to a fluorescent cuvette. The fluorescent intensity of the solution was recorded from 260 to 520nm with excitation wavelength fixed at 340nm. Afterwards the prepared Cu2+ solution was added into 2mL T-GQDs solutions according to a series of concentration gradient, which was then slowly shaken up and stood still for 3min. Then the same PL test was performed for the mixture solution. In addition, similar procedure was conducted for other metal ions, including Fe3+, Fe2+, Au2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Ni+, Sr2+, Co+, Al3+, Li+, Ba2+, K+ and Na+. The ion concentration of all these mixed solutions was kept 30μM. All measurements were made at room temperature.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a JEM-2010 transmission electron microscope. FT-IR spectra were recorded on a NEXUS spectrometer 670. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured by an ESCALAB250 Xi photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher). Raman spectra were recorded on a LabRAM HR Evolution with a 514.5nm laser. The UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained by a UNIC UV-2800 spectrophotometer. The PL spectra were conducted by an F-4500 FL Spectrophotometer with a slit width of 5nm. The excitation wavelength started from 260nm and progressively increased to longer excitation wavelengths in 20nm increments.

As shown in Fig. 1 a–f, we choose a series of doses of K2S2O8 to do the oxidized experiments. According to the almost unchanged PL emission of Fig. 1b and little change in PL color 1 For interpretation of color in Fig. 1, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.
1 of Fig. 1c, as well as the nearly quenched fluorescence (Fig. 1e and f) of the post-treated GQDs, it is obvious that 20μL K2S2O8 solution is suitable to oxidize GQDs solution with the concentration of 0.05mg/ml, because of the obvious change in PL color from green to greenish-yellow. More or less K2S2O8 solution may lead to the suboxidation or overoxidation of GQDs. Therefore, we choose 20μL K2S2O8 to do the oxidized experiments.

As shown in Fig. 2 , the TEM images of GQDs and T-GQDs exhibited their spherical shapes and excellent monodispersity. From the histograms of size distributions, it can be seen that the size of the as-prepared GQDs were determined to be approximately 1–4nm, with an average diameter of 2.4nm, while T-GQDs maintained the original morphology, size as well as the size distribution after post-oxidation treatment, suggesting that the approach was a mild strategy for GQDs modification and didn’t destroy the intrinsic structure of GQDs.

Fig. 3 a showed the XPS spectra of GQDs and T-GQDs. As can be seen from the figure, it exhibited two typical C1s and O1s peaks at 285.4eV and 532eV [25], indicating that no other element was introduced during the whole process. In addition, the oxygen content of GQDs was 31.40%, while that of T-GQDs was 43.96%. The increase of oxygen content confirmed an oxidation reaction which occurred between GQDs and K2S2O8 under 365nm UV irradiation. The FT-IR spectra of the GQDs and T-GQDs were given in Fig. 3b. It showed similar kinds of oxygen functional groups on surface of GQDs and T-GQDs. For both spectra, the peaks at 1047–1117cm−1 and 1643cm−1 are attributed to epoxy (COC) and carbonyl groups (CO), respectively [26,27]. The bands at around 1440cm−1 correspond to the carboxyl groups (COOH), and the peaks at around 3422cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups (OH) [28]. As it was expected, the COOH vibration peak of T-GQDs was intensified compared with that of GQDs. Besides, the intensity of CO, COC and OH vibration peaks all increased to varying degrees, which was consistent with the increase of oxygen content mentioned in the previous XPS results. The Raman spectra were shown in Fig. 3c, both showed two main peaks at approximately 1345cm−1 (D-band) and 1595cm−1 (G-band) [25]. As is known, the ratio of ID/IG in Raman spectra is used to evaluate the disorder in graphene materials, since the D band is related to the structural defects and partially disordered structures of the sp2 domains, and the G band depends on the E2g vibration mode of the sp2 carbon domains [25]. It can be calculated that the ID/IG of T-GQDs was 0.86, significantly larger than that of GQDs (0.56). The increase of surface defect was caused by the increase of oxygen functional groups on GQDs’ surface. Thus the results further demonstrated that GQDs were successfully oxidized by K2S2O8 under UV light.

Fig. 4 a displayed the UV–visible absorption spectra of GQDs and T-GQDs. A strong absorption peak of the pristine GQDs at 216nm which corresponded to the characteristic π-π∗ electron transitions of sp2 state and a shoulder at about 288nm which was attributed to n-π∗ transitions of CO bonds of sp3 state are observed [25]. The absorption behavior for T-GQDs exhibited a red-shift from 216nm to 224nm indicating that the size of π conjugated region increased.

As shown in Fig. 4b–c, it displayed the PL spectra of GQDs and T-GQDs under different excitation wavelengths, which were of significant differences. Similar to other luminescent carbon nanoparticles, GQDs exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence emission, while a new emission peak appeared between 330 and 340nm in the PL spectra of T-GQDs as the excitation wavelength changed. Moreover, it revealed a phenomenon that multiple emission peaks dominated the PL spectra.

To further analysis the differences between GQDs and T-GQDs in PL properties, the PL spectra were deconvoluted into several peaks under different excitation wavelengths as shown in Fig. 5 a–h. These PL emissions can be mainly divided into 3 emission peaks: peak 1 locating at ∼440nm represented the blue emission, peak 2 centering at ∼490nm represented the light green emission while peak 3 at ∼540nm represented greenish-yellow emission; those three peaks matched the curve very well. In addition, a new emission peak appeared in the ultraviolet zone (around 330nm) in the PL spectra of T-GQDs under short wavelength excitation. The integrated area of peak 1 of GQDs and T-GQDs decreased in proportion as the excitation wavelength increased, demonstrating the blue emission became weaker at the same time, while the integrated area of peak 3 which represented greenish-yellow emission increased in proportion contrarily. All of them indicated that different emission groups or locations dominated the fluorescence emission as the excitation wavelength changed. From aforementioned results, the sizes and concentration of GQDs and T-GQDs were basically the same, while the XPS results showed different oxygen content degree, thus it can be inferred that the different luminescent properties should be attributed to different content of each oxygen function group on the surface of GQDs. Accompanied with the formation of various oxygen functional groups, surface defect with high concentration trapped more excitons to hold back intrinsic state emission [29]. When a certain excitation wavelength illuminated the GQDs, some of excited photoelectrons would relax to the energy level of surface state and then be back to the ground state to emission fluorescence. As the excitation wavelength changed, different surface state traps will dominate the emission of GQDs [29], thus the excitation-dependent emission was observed. With the further oxidation on T-GQDs’ surface and the increase of various oxygen functional groups, the excited photoelectrons were the most possibly to relax to the corresponding defect energy levels and then turned back to the ground state through radiative recombination, thus the phenomenon that PL spectra were dominated by multiple luminescence peak appeared [29]. Besides, it can be seen from Fig. 5e–h that the new emission peak appeared in the ultraviolet zone may correspond to the triplet excited σ-π∗ transition of sp3 hybrid carbon [30]. With the change of excitation wavelength, the proportion of new emission area increased first and then decreased in the whole PL spectra, and the emission peak red-shifted gradually from 331nm to 340nm. Excited by the wavelength of 300nm, the corresponding PL emission at 335nm showed the highest intensity in the ultraviolet zone. It is the shortest wavelength of near-ultraviolet emission apart from the deep ultraviolet emission (4.10eV) of GQDs reported by Tang et al. [29].

Based on its high oxidation degree, the detection capability of T-GQDs for Cu2+ was tested. According to the PL spectra of T-GQDs, the wavelength of 340nm was chosen as the excitation wavelength, at which T-GQDs displayed the maximum emission intensity as shown in Fig. 4c. Moreover, because the added volume of the K2S2O8 solution was small compared to the GQDs solution, GQDs and T-GQDs could be considered as having the same concentration of 0.05mg/ml. It can be seen from Fig. 6 a that Cu2+ effectively quenched the fluorescence of T-GQDs, and the fluorescence intensity decreased to about 30% of its initial value when the concentration of Cu2+ reached 30μM. When the concentration of Cu2+ reached 130μM, the fluorescence intensity of T-GQDs solution became very weak and its value reached a fixed level. Particularly, with the addition of Cu2+, an obvious blue shift phenomenon was observed, which indicated the complexation between Cu2+ and T-GQDs (according to the report by Wang et al. [31]). The occurrence of complexation will lead to charge or energy transfer between Cu2+ and T-GQDs, thus quenching the fluorescence of T-GQDs [22]. Meanwhile, it can be found that Cu2+ can even be detected by T-GQDs when the concentration of Cu2+ was down to 2μM, which satisfactorily met the limit of Cu2+ in drinking water (∼20μM) set by U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, thus making it possible to be used in the practical Cu2+ detection [6].

As shown in Fig. 6b, it clearly demonstrated the relationship between the concentration of Cu2+ and the fluorescence intensity of T-GQDs. F/F0, the fluorescence intensity ratio of complex Cu2+-T-GQDs and T-GQDs excited by 340nm wavelength, decreased as the concentration of Cu2+ increased. Its reducing rate became slower and tended to be stable within 20–130μM, and the relation between the quenching degree of T-GQDs and the concentration of Cu2+ within 0–20μM was further explicated. The regression coefficient R2 was calculated to 0.9904, which was close to 1, indicating that it was a near-linear relation between the fluorescence quenching degree and the concentration of Cu2+. Furthermore, at low concentrations of 0–20μM, the quenching effect can be quantitatively described by the Stern–Volmer equation [32]:
(1)
F
0
/ F = 1 +
K
SV
[ C ]
where F0 is the fluorescence intensity without Cu2+, F is the fluorescence intensity observed in the presence of Cu2+ and KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant. The Stern-Volmer plots in Fig. 7 were almost linear, which indicated the high quenching efficiency of Cu2+ to the T-GQDs.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of Cu2+ detection, same fluorescent quenching procedures were conducted for other common ions which may interfere with this detection method. These ions are mainly associated with the environment, including Fe3+, Fe2+, Au2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Ni+, Sr2+, Co+, Al3+, Li+, Ba2+, K+ and Na+. The concentration of each kind of ion was set as 30μM and the excitation wavelength was 340nm. It can be seen from Fig. 8 that compared with other metal ions, Cu2+ weakened the fluorescence intensity of T-GQDs most. The initial fluorescence intensity of T-GQDs tripled that of complex Cu2+-T-GQDs, while the F/F0 of T-GQDs added with other ions was mostly below 1.5, much smaller than that of complex Cu2+-T-GQDs, suggesting that T-GQDs solution is sensitive for the detection of Cu2+.

This paper adopted a universal, mild and in situ post treatment method to modify the prepared GQDs, thus generating more carboxyl groups on their surface which can present better response for Cu2+. When the concentration of Cu2+ reached 30μM, the PL intensity of T-GQDs was only 30% of the initial value, much lower than that of other metal ions. Moreover, the fluorescence quenching degree and the concentration of Cu2+ have shown a good linear relation within the concentration range of 0–20μM, so T-GQDs can detect the level of Cu2+ sensitively. It is hoped that this work can be beneficial to the modification of GQDs and widen its application in fluorescence detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21674011, 21374009, 51202011), Beijing Municipal Natural Science Foundation (2132049), Beijing Organization Department Outstanding Talented Person Project (2013D009006000001) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (FRF-TP-15-005A3). Yan Li is thankful for the financial support from the program of the China Scholarship Council (CSC).
